title: Targeting Clause Type Distributions: a Picklock for Random Satisfiability Problems
abstract: Optimization problems such as the NP-complete 3-SAT provide an important benchmark for the difficult task of finding ground-states in strongly correlated many-body systems with rugged energy landscapes. The study of random 3-SAT problems as Ising spin Hamiltonians in statistical physics has yielded major insights including the existence of a satisfiability phase transition, and the prediction of a critical parameter line of particularly hard instances. Yet, progress on solving those instances has been scarce for several decades. Here, introducing the Target-SAT (TSAT) algorithm, we roughly triple the tractable problem sizes in the hardest regime, with an even greater improvement in a vast range of neighboring regions. By leveraging statistical information hidden in the combinatorial constraints of the problem, TSAT is actively guided in its stochastic local search toward a target within the relevant parameter space. Our analysis also explains why established local search algorithms are limited to relatively small system sizes due to a vast low-energy trap. Furthermore, we characterize the aforementioned critical line in terms of a dominant additional complexity barrier, whose exponential scaling is quickly overcome by TSAT only in the surrounding parameter space. With TSAT, the lead in solving the hardest known random satisfiability problems returns to the realm of stochastic local search algorithms.
summary: The paper presents a new algorithm, Target-SAT, that significantly improves the ability to solve the hardest instances of random 3-SAT problems. By navigating through a specific parameter space of clause distributions, the algorithm overcomes a major complexity barrier that previously limited stochastic local search methods. This provides new insights into the landscape of computational hardness in frustrated systems.
main_problem: Overcoming the computational hardness of random 3-SAT instances in the most difficult regime, characterized by a critical line of hard instances and a vast low-energy trap.
main_result: The introduction of the Target-SAT (TSAT) algorithm, which roughly triples the tractable problem size in the hardest regime and identifies a specific complexity barrier in the clause-type distribution space.
method: A new Stochastic Local Search (SLS) heuristic called TSAT that uses statistical information from the Clause-Type Distribution Space (CTDS) to actively guide the search toward a target distribution.
model_or_system: Random 3-SAT problems mapped to Ising spin Hamiltonians, representing strongly correlated many-body systems with rugged energy landscapes and frustrated magnetic interactions.
key_observables: Clause-Type Distribution (CTD), average success probability, energy (number of unsatisfied clauses), and scaling of runtime.
important_parameters: Clause density (alpha), clause-type distribution ratios (r1, r2), system size (N), and the critical parameter line.
main_assumptions: The study focuses on random 3-SAT instances; the effectiveness of the algorithm on highly structured or practical non-random problems is not yet established.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows search trajectories in the CTDS; Figure 2 compares success probabilities of Walksat and Tsat; Figure 3 shows scaling of success probability with N; Figure 4 shows the emergence of complexity barriers in state histograms; Figure 7 compares solved problem ratios across different regimes.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of 3-SAT hardness, presents the TSAT algorithm, compares its performance against Walksat and complete solvers, analyzes the complexity barriers in the CTDS, and discusses scaling and limitations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Illustration of Tsat and Walksat trajectories through clause-type distribution space (CTDS), starting from random initial states. The color corresponds to the log10 of the density of states (DoS), and for the Tsat trajectories we also show the accumulated DoS on the left (cf. Fig. 4). The grayscale projections are along the E and r2 axes and serve as a visual guide. Walksat does not reach the solution at E = 0 because it gets stuck in a vast local minimum of low-energy states (trap). The Tsat algorithm introduced in this article takes a completely different path and encounters a barrier be- fore entering a final search for the solution located near the bottom left.

FIG. 2. Average success probability ⟨p⟩of Walksat (left panel) and Tsat (right panel) for problems generated via Al- gorithm 2. At each point in CTDS we generate 102 instances with N = 1000 and α = 5, using 102 trials per instance and 300N flips per trial (cf. Appendix C for solver specific parameters). Even on the clearly distinguished critical line r2 = 3
FIG. 3. (a) Average success probability ⟨p⟩of an individual trial for Walksat, Docsat and Tsat for A = (0.7, 0.1) (103

FIG. 5. Setup as in Fig. 4, but at B = (0.65, 0.1). Away from the critical line the initial barrier appears to increase only linearly (note that the number of bins along the x-axis is ∝N, hence the coalescence of all lines). This explains why Tsat retains an average ⟨p⟩≈1 in Fig. 3(b) up to very large N; beyond N ≈5000 the final search phase becomes the bottleneck (see crossing in the inset), and thus eventually inhibits solubility.

FIG. 4. For varying size N, we run Tsat on 102 instances at A = (0.7, 0.1) for 102 trials each, with unlimited flips per trial (until a solution is found). The plot shows a histogram of the number of states x found at a certain distance-to-target (cf. Eq. 7), with random initial states situated on the right and solutions near ∆CTDS ≈0 on the left. With increasing N, two peaks emerge and the search is dominated by the time it takes to traverse the barrier at ∆CTDS/M ≈0.5. Outside the shaded regions marking the peaks all lines coalesce, indi- cating that navigating the CTDS takes O(N) time in those regions.
